## PR summary
<!-- Please provide at least 1-2 sentences describing the pull request in detail

Also please summarize the changes in the title, for example "Raise ValueError on

The existing type definition for `xycoords` (and friends) only support passing a tuple of floats, where in fact a tuple of anything that is valid on its own is acceptable.  The easiest way to define this is with type variables, which I then reused everywhere.

I defined this [based on the docs here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/_as_gen/matplotlib.pyplot.annotate.html#matplotlib.pyplot.annotate), not really from reading the code, for what it's worth.

There is an interesting recent paper about exposing the bytecode interpreter specialization features added in Python 3.11 (aka quickening) to arbitrary extension types.
 
https://drops.dagstuhl.de/entities/document/10.4230/LIPIcs.ECOOP.2024.6

For background, the bytecode specialization work for Python 3.11 essentially amounts to: if we notice that a generic instruction `BINARY_OP_ADD` is being called from a hot loop, replace it with an instruction that is specialized for the specific types that are present at the call site, e.g. `BINARY_OP_ADD_INT`.  This concept is the bulk of what gave Python 3.11 its ~30% speed improvement.

The innovation in the paper is to expose this facility to arbitrary extension types, so that a type can cache the results of dealing with dynamism at each call site and then skip that work on subsequent calls.  In the case of Numpy, this is all the "work planning" of handling casting, broadcasting, overrided ufuncs etc. that *usually* (but not always) doesn't change between calls at the same call site.  A key finding of the paper is that this overhead is enough, especially in the case of small to medium sized arrays, that the performance improvements can be very significant, on the order of more than 2x.

Prototype patches against CPython and Numpy used for the paper are available here:

Changes to Numpy: https://github.com/fberlakovich/cmq-numpy-ae
Changes to CPython: https://github.com/fberlakovich/cmq-ae

As a member of the Faster CPython team, I'd like to work on making this a reality, but the majority of the work actually needs to happen in Numpy, and I could use some help on that side and would also like to gauge the interest of the Numpy developers first.  (I've made modest contributions to Numpy in the past, but I definitely no longer have my bearings).  Looking at the patch to Numpy, you can see it involves generating code to handle and specialize many of the cases.  The authors used their own mako templates to do this, but they admit in the paper that it would perhaps be better to leverage the code generation that exists already.

What do you all think is the best way to proceed?  Would this require a NEP?

(For reference some [earlier discussion in the faster-cpython/ideas tracker](https://github.com/faster-cpython/ideas/issues/696)).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

I think the major gains from this approach would only happen when both CPython and Numpy are participating in adaptive specalization.  So that would be CPython 3.14 at the earliest.  There may of course be other ways to optimize how numpy does these things globally, but the "magic insight" in the paper is that by caching things at each particular call site you can do much better.

I think the easiest path forward would be for me to get @fberlakovich's fork on CPython working on top CPython main. The existing type definition for `xycoords` (and friends) only support passing a tuple of floats, where in fact a tuple of anything that is valid on its own is acceptable.  The easiest way to define this is with type variables, which I then reused everywhere.

I defined this [based on the docs here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/_as_gen/matplotlib.pyplot.annotate.html#matplotlib.pyplot.annotate), not really from reading the code, for what it's worth.

growth of [WebAssembly]( https://webassembly.org/ ) is [really quite impressive]( https://blog.mozilla.org/blog/2017/11/13/webassembly-in-browsers/ ). It wasn't that long ago when [asm.js]( http://asmjs.org/ ) was what people targeted for performant browsers apps. There has been work on [SIMD]( https://github.com/WebAssembly/simd ), [threading]( https://github.com/WebAssembly/threads ) support for WebAssembly. Now [many browsers]( https://caniuse.com/wasm ) support WebAssembly out-of-the-box.

The growth of compilers and interpreters in WebAssembly is also quite impressive. [Emscripten emits WebAssembly by default]( https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/pull/6419 ). There have been efforts to add [WebAssembly to the GNU toolchain]( https://sourceware.org/ml/binutils/2017-03/msg00044.html ). [Binaryen]( https://kripken.github.io/talks/binaryen.html#/ ) provides a nice toolchain/compiler for WebAssembly, which can used with Emscripten & LLVM or by itself. [Several language implementations]( https://github.com/appcypher/awesome-wasm-langs ) have been ported or implemented in WebAssembly.

On the user facing front, there has even been success at getting [Python with NumPy]( http://droettboom.com/blog/2018/04/04/python-in-the-browser/ ) working in WebAssembly. Not to mention [Qt has WebAssembly support]( http://blog.qt.io/blog/2018/05/22/qt-for-webassembly/ ). [AutoCAD has a web version that has been ported to WebAssembly]( http://www.keanw.com/2018/03/the-future-of-autocad.html ). All of this really demonstrates the power of this technology and the doors that it opens. Though it also raises a really big problem as well.

Namely how do we build, package, and manage the stacks of software that can and will be used in WebAssembly in the future and how can we use this powerful tool to do better science in the future? Of course Emscripten can be used to do this, but [packaging Emscripten is hard]( https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/issues/6015 ). Not to mention it relies on things like LLVM that are also hard to package. Also there are a few other toolchain choices, so what is the right one to pick? So getting a toolchain going that can be used to easily build packages is the first challenge.

The next challenge is understanding how best to package things. Does everything get "statically linked" into one huge blob or is there a good strategy for declaring dependencies? How do we specify ABI compatibility of components? How do we constrain the target architecture of a WebAssembly built package from other architectures (e.g. macOS, Linux, or Windows)? Also when it comes to the web, [size is really important]( https://hacks.mozilla.org/2018/01/shrinking-webassembly-and-javascript-code-sizes-in-emscripten/ ). So figuring out the best optimizations to [shrink package size as much as possible]( https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/issues/6904 ) is critical for success.

I don't have good answers for these questions, but it seems clear this is an important area that we should start investigating. Interested in hearing thoughts and suggestions on how we might best explore this space.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/7619/reactions","total_count":18,"+1":13,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":5,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/7619/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/comments/1018836071","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/7619#issuecomment-1018836071","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/7619","id":1018836071,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAF8kJs48ujRn","user":{"login":"mdboom","id":38294,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM4Mjk0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/38294?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom","html_url":"https://github.com/mdboom","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-21T20:28:26Z","updated_at":"2022-01-21T20:28:26Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"(Pardon my ignorance of how Conda works under the hood) -- since packages are already parameterized by the Python version (for ABI reasons), could they also be parameterized by emscripten version? Not to mention [Qt has WebAssembly support]( http://blog.qt.io/blog/2018/05/22/qt-for-webassembly/ ). [AutoCAD has a web version that has been ported to WebAssembly]( http://www.keanw.com/2018/03/the-future-of-autocad.html ). All of this really demonstrates the power of this technology and the doors that it opens. Though it also raises a really big problem as well.

Namely how do we build, package, and manage the stacks of software that can and will be used in WebAssembly in the future and how can we use this powerful tool to do better science in the future? Of course Emscripten can be used to do this, but [packaging Emscripten is hard]( https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/issues/6015 ). Not to mention it relies on things like LLVM that are also hard to package. Also there are a few other toolchain choices, so what is the right one to pick? So getting a toolchain going that can be used to easily build packages is the first challenge.

The next challenge is understanding how best to package things. Does everything get "statically linked" into one huge blob or is there a good strategy for declaring dependencies? How do we specify ABI compatibility of components? How do we constrain the target architecture of a WebAssembly built package from other architectures (e.g. macOS, Linux, or Windows)? Also when it comes to the web, [size is really important]( https://hacks.mozilla.org/2018/01/shrinking-webassembly-and-javascript-code-sizes-in-emscripten/ ). So figuring out the best optimizations to [shrink package size as much as possible]( https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/issues/6904 ) is critical for success.

I don't have good answers for these questions, but it seems clear this is an important area that we should start investigating.
